title: SEGMENT 9 — Payload classes — tubulin, DNA damage, topoisomerase

lines.text: SEGMENT 9 — Payload classes — tubulin, DNA damage, topoisomerase
Now let's take a tour of the actual warheads.
The payload is the cytotoxic molecule that does the killing once it's released.
ADC payloads fall into three big functional classes today, plus a few emerging classes that we'll touch on in the final segment.
The three big classes are tubulin inhibitors, DNA-damaging agents, and topoisomerase I inhibitors.
Let me walk through each one with examples.
Tubulin inhibitors are the most common ADC payload class historically.
They work by binding to the protein tubulin, which is the building block of microtubules — the filaments that form the mitotic spindle when a cell divides.
When a cell tries to divide, it needs to assemble microtubules to pull its duplicated chromosomes apart into the two daughter cells.
If tubulin is jammed up by an inhibitor, the spindle can't form, the chromosomes can't separate, the cell arrests in mitosis, and eventually triggers apoptosis.
There are two famous sub-families.
The auristatins — MMAE and MMAF — derived from a sea slug natural product called dolastatin.
MMAE is the cleavable-linker-friendly version; MMAF has a charge that makes it membrane-impermeable and is therefore typically paired with non-cleavable linkers.
Adcetris uses MMAE.
So do enfortumab vedotin (Padcev, for urothelial cancer), polatuzumab vedotin (Polivy, for diffuse large B-cell lymphoma), and several others.
The maytansinoids — DM1 and DM4 — derived from an Ethiopian shrub.
They bind tubulin at a different site than the auristatins.
T-DM1 uses DM1.
Mirvetuximab soravtansine, an ADC against folate receptor alpha for ovarian cancer, uses DM4.
Tubulin inhibitor payloads are mature technology.
We've been using them for over a decade.
We know their toxicities, their pharmacokinetics, their interactions.
But they have limitations.
They only kill dividing cells — cells in interphase, not actively dividing, are relatively resistant.
And tumors can develop resistance through several well-characterized mechanisms.
DNA-damaging agents are the second class.
These are payloads that bind to DNA itself, or to DNA-associated proteins, and generate damage that the cell can't repair.
The most famous example is calicheamicin, the payload used in Mylotarg.

Calicheamicin binds in the minor groove of DNA and, through a unique chemistry involving an enediyne functional group, generates two carbon-centered radicals that simultaneously abstract hydrogen atoms from each strand of the DNA double helix, creating a double-strand break.
The chemistry is genuinely beautiful and genuinely terrifying.
Calicheamicin is one of the most potent cytotoxins ever isolated, with picomolar potency.
Other DNA-damaging payloads include the pyrrolobenzodiazepines, or PBDs, which form covalent cross-links in DNA.
Loncastuximab tesirine, an anti-CD19 ADC for diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, uses a PBD dimer.
These molecules are even more potent than calicheamicin in some cellular assays — sub-picomolar activity — and the resulting ADCs have shown impressive single-agent activity in lymphomas.

The downside of DNA-damaging payloads is that they tend to have characteristic toxicities — particularly to bone marrow, where cells are constantly dividing and the DNA repair machinery is heavily engaged.
They also tend to be mutagenic in their own right, which raises long-term safety concerns.
The use of these payloads has therefore been more limited than tubulin inhibitors, despite their potency.
Topoisomerase I inhibitors are the third class, and they're the rising stars.
Topoisomerase I is an enzyme that relaxes DNA supercoiling during replication and transcription by transiently nicking one strand of the DNA, allowing rotation, and then religating the nick.
Topo I inhibitors — like camptothecin and its derivatives irinotecan, topotecan, and exatecan — bind to the transient enzyme-DNA complex and prevent religation, leaving a single-strand break behind.
When a replication fork hits one of these stuck complexes, it converts to a double-strand break, which kills the cell.
The ADC version of this class is DXd, the deruxtecan payload in T-DXd, derived from exatecan.
The ADC version in SN-38 is the active metabolite of irinotecan, which is the payload in sacituzumab govitecan (Trodelvy).
We'll discuss Trodelvy in detail in Segment 13.
Topo I inhibitors have several attractive properties for ADC payloads.
They're potent — picomolar to low nanomolar.
They kill non-dividing cells better than tubulin inhibitors do, because topo I is engaged not just in replication but also in transcription.
They have relatively manageable toxicity profiles — the main dose-limiting toxicity tends to be neutropenia and diarrhea, both of which are reversible.
And they appear to be effective against tumors that have become resistant to tubulin inhibitor ADCs, because the mechanism of cell killing is completely different.
The rise of topo I payloads is one of the major stories of the last five years of ADC development.
Almost every new ADC program in the clinic now involves either a topo I payload or, less commonly, a novel payload class we'll discuss in Segment 18.
So that's the warhead taxonomy.
Tubulin inhibitors for mature applications.
DNA-damaging agents for high-potency specialty uses.
Topo I inhibitors for the new wave of broadly applicable, bystander-capable ADCs.
Three classes, dozens of specific molecules, and the toolkit keeps growing.
If you're delivering this as a lecture, this is the place to take a ten-minute break.

We've covered the central pharmacology, the three components, the early history including Mylotarg, the two anti-HER2 drugs that defined the field's modern era, the bystander effect, and the chemistry of linkers and payloads.

After the break, we'll look at the engineering details — DAR, site-specific conjugation, the broader pipeline, additional drugs like Trodelvy, the clinical realities of toxicity and resistance, manufacturing, and where the field is heading.
Stretch your legs.
Get some water.
We'll continue in ten.
